contraindications: Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: NCCIH lists adverse effects (dizziness, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, muscle pain) and notes uncertain pregnancy/lactation safety; one study suggested a specific hawthorn preparation may have increased early risk of heart-failure progression (mechanism unclear, possible interaction with concurrent drugs). LiverTox describes hawthorn as generally well tolerated with mostly minor GI adverse effects. FDA’s warning about substituted "tejocote root" products adds a practical safety screening need (verify product identity).

sources.title: FDA warning about tejocote root supplements substituted with toxic yellow oleander

description: A flowering shrub/tree in the rose family, occurring in temperate regions; hawthorn is an umbrella term spanning multiple *Crataegus* species in commerce and traditional use.

interactions: NCCIH suggests a possible interaction explanation in the study reporting early heart-failure progression risk; otherwise, specific interaction pairs are not established on the NCCIH page, so a cautious "review meds" note is appropriate.
preparation: Typically sold as hawthorn supplements and "tejocote root" products; given documented substitution risk, preparation notes should emphasize verified identity.
region: Hawthorn broadly occurs in temperate regions worldwide (general term). As an example species commonly referenced in commerce, POWO lists *Crataegus monogyna* native range as Europe to Caucasus, N. Africa to Iraq.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for mild digestive comfort
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: Hawthorn is promoted for heart/blood-vessel health and other uses; NCCIH reports conflicting evidence in heart-failure studies and insufficient evidence for other cardiovascular endpoints. Short-term safety may be acceptable for some products, but data beyond ~16 weeks are lacking. A major safety/regulatory concern is product substitution/adulteration: FDA has warned that some products labeled as tejocote (Mexican hawthorn) root were adulterated with toxic yellow oleander.
